planting.note: Plant grafted or air-layered trees in spring; in zone 10 plant March–June. Sapodilla is best suited to zones 10–11 but can be grown in protected zone 9b microclimates. Established trees tolerate brief temperatures to 27–28°F; young trees are damaged below 32°F. Brown Sugar is the preferred cultivar in South Florida for home orchards due to its superior flavor and consistent production. Trees fruit throughout the year with peaks in spring and fall.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, alkaline-tolerant soil — sapodilla is one of the few tropical fruits that tolerates the alkaline, rocky soils of South Florida; Brown Sugar is a Florida-selected sapodilla cultivar named for its extraordinary sweetness and distinctive brown-sugar-molasses flavor that sets it apart from standard sapodilla varieties. The medium-sized, round to slightly oval, brown-skinned fruits have grainy, sandy-textured, translucent tan flesh that is so sweet it rivals candy — one of the sweetest fruits grown in North America, with a caramel-brown-sugar-molasses flavor that is universally appreciated even by people unfamiliar with the species. Sapodilla latex in the flesh (chicle, historically the base of chewing gum) gives the texture a slightly sticky, granular quality that is distinctive. Brown Sugar is considered the finest-flavored sapodilla cultivar for fresh eating and is the recommended variety for home orchards in zone 10 South Florida. The evergreen, windproof tree is also extremely drought-tolerant once established.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sapodilla Brown Sugar Cultivar
University of Florida Tropical Research and Education Center — Sapodilla Varieties
California Rare Fruit Growers — Sapodilla Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; in zone 9 plant March–May. Carambola trees are frost-sensitive — young trees are damaged below 30°F and established trees can be killed at 25°F. In zone 9a, plant in the most frost-protected microclimate available (south-facing walls, under taller trees, or in containers that can be moved indoors). Zone 9b provides better conditions; zone 10 is ideal. Arkin fruits primarily in August–November and again in March–June, providing two annual harvest periods.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Arkin is the leading commercial carambola cultivar in Florida, selected by the University of Florida for its combination of large fruit size, sweet flavor, and attractive appearance — the five-lobed, waxy, golden-yellow fruits are the classic star-fruit appearance when sliced crosswise, producing the familiar star-shaped cross-sections used to garnish drinks, fruit platters, and Asian dishes. Unlike the sour Malaysian varieties (planted for processing), Arkin is a sweet variety with a balanced sweet-tart flavor suitable for fresh eating, juicing, and garnishing. The self-fertile tree is moderately productive even as a single plant, producing 200–400 pounds annually in good conditions. Carambola is high in vitamin C and very low in sugar compared to most tropical fruits, making it popular in health-conscious cuisines. In zone 9 the tree requires careful siting to avoid frost damage — plant against south-facing structures for maximum cold protection.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Arkin Carambola Cultivar Release
University of Florida Tropical Research and Education Center — Carambola Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Star Fruit for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant March–May. Manila Tamarind is more cold-hardy than most tropical fruits — established trees survive light frosts to approximately 24–26°F and quickly resprout from the roots if frozen back. In zone 9 the tree is semi-deciduous in cold winters. The twisted, ornamental seed pods ripen from December through May. Trees may naturalize in zone 9 conditions and should be sited where fruit drop is not a nuisance.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, poor-to-fertile soil — Manila Tamarind is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, sandy, even compacted soils where most fruit trees fail; Manila Tamarind (also known as guamúchil, Madras thorn, or camachile) is a thorny, tropical legume tree from Mexico and Central America that produces coiled, bright red-and-white seed pods containing sweet, fibrous white pulp surrounding shiny black seeds. The sweet-tart, cotton-candy-like flavor of the fresh white aril (the edible fleshy layer around the seeds) is popular in Mexican street food markets and throughout tropical Asia where the tree has naturalized. Despite the common name, Manila Tamarind is not related to true tamarind (Tamarindus indica) but belongs to the legume family with nitrogen-fixing roots. The tree is highly productive and drought-tolerant, making it valuable for edible landscape use in hot, dry zone 9 conditions. Pods are harvested when the husks split open to reveal the red-pink exterior and white interior with sweet pulp.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Manila Tamarind
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pithecellobium dulce in South Texas
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Exotic Edible Trees for Gulf South

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Capsicum chinense requires warm temperatures (80–90°F) for germination and is slow to germinate. In zone 9 start in January and transplant in March–April. Butch T thrives as a perennial in zone 9 and 10 — the woody plants return from the roots after light winter die-back and produce extremely heavily in their second and subsequent years. Wear gloves when handling fruits; the extremely high capsaicin content can cause skin burns and eye damage.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil and handle fruits with protective gloves; Butch T Trinidad Scorpion was recognized as the world's hottest pepper by the Guinness World Records in 2011 (since surpassed by the Carolina Reaper), measuring over 1.4 million Scoville Heat Units in official testing — placing it among the most intensely hot peppers ever documented. The variety was developed by Troy Primeaux ('Butch T') in Louisiana from Trinidad Moruga Scorpion genetics. The small to medium, wrinkled, deeply colored red-orange fruits have the distinctive scorpion tail-like protrusion at the tip that gives the Trinidad Scorpion type its name. The extreme heat is accompanied by a pronounced fruity, tropical Capsicum chinense flavor beneath the intense burn. Used in tiny quantities in hot sauce production, powders, and as a daring culinary challenge. In zone 9 the long warm season and perennial habit allow Butch T plants to develop into large, extremely productive multi-year shrubs.
sources: Guinness World Records — Butch T Scorpion Pepper 2011 Record
New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute — Super-Hot Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Specialty Pepper Production

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed. In zone 9 start in January–February and transplant in February–March for a spring crop. Stake or cage heavily — Pink Ponderosa vines are tall (5–7 feet) and produce very large, heavy fruits that require solid support. In zone 9 a fall planting in August for October–November harvest often produces better-quality fruit with fewer cracks.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil and provide sturdy staking; Pink Ponderosa (also called 'Henderson's Pink Ponderosa') is a late 19th-century American heirloom tomato introduced by the Henderson seed company around 1891, noted for producing enormous, beefsteak-type fruits of a distinctive deep rose-pink color — the 'pink' refers to the red flesh showing through the colorless (no yellow pigment in skin) skin, creating the characteristic pink-red appearance of traditional beefsteak-type heirlooms. The very large fruits (often 1–2 pounds each) are meaty, low-acid, and juicy with a mild, sweet, rich tomato flavor that made Ponderosa-type tomatoes the standard fresh-eating tomato for most of the 20th century before uniform-color commercial hybrids replaced them. The indeterminate vine produces continuously until frost and can be especially productive in zone 9's long growing season. The mild, low-acid flavor makes Pink Ponderosa particularly appealing for sliced tomatoes where the full fruit flavor is experienced without cooking.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Pink Ponderosa Heirloom Tomato
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Pink Ponderosa History
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heirloom Tomato Production Guide
